story_shape: the five-star who changed zip codes -- Auburn's flashes, Arch Manning's quarterback, and the breakout that's been waiting for the right thrower

plan.spine: a top-5 recruit who flashed at Auburn through a coaching change transfers to Texas to become Arch Manning's go-to target -- the talent was never the question, the quarterback was
blend: No. 5 overall in the 2024 class; No. 2 player in the 2026 portal
1,306 receiving yards in two Auburn seasons (708 in 2025)
transferred to Texas specifically to pair with Arch Manning
6-3, 201 -- the contested-catch profile a vertical offense wants
plan.payoff: 708 yards through a coaching change in 2025; now paired with a blue-chip quarterback and two years of eligibility
plan.forward_stake: whether elite quarterback play finally unlocks the five-star ceiling -- the difference between a good receiver and a 2027 first-round profile

hook: Cam Coleman was a five-star out of high school and the No. 2 player in the transfer portal. He left Auburn for one reason: to catch passes from Arch Manning. The talent was never the question -- the quarterback was.
expand: He was the No. 5 overall recruit in the 2024 class, and even through a 2025 coaching change at Auburn he put up 708 yards -- 1,306 across his two seasons, third-most by an Auburn receiver through a sophomore year. Then he became the marquee move of the offseason: portal's No. 2 player, off to Texas to be Arch Manning's WR1 in Manning's first full year as the unquestioned starter. At 6-3, 201 he's the catch-radius target a vertical passing game is built to feed, and he joins a room that still returns Ryan Wingo. The forward stakes are the swing of the whole bet: a five-star whose production always hinted at more than it showed now has the quarterback to prove it. For Coleman, 2026 is the breakout that's been waiting on the throw -- and the line between a good college receiver and a top-of-the-2027-board profile.
style: Coleman wins above the rim. At 6-3, 201 he's a jump-ball and contested-catch specialist -- On3's scouting deep-dive calls him a receiver who "plays above the rim," "climbs the ladder and boxes out defenders," and finishes through contact, a "red-zone nightmare with an exceptional catch radius." Our play-by-play backs the vertical profile: his explosive-catch rate graded in the 72nd percentile, the chunk-play element of a downfield target, even as his overall efficiency (mid-pack yards-per-reception and touchdown rate) marks a player whose production hadn't yet caught up to the talent. The honest edge, per the same report, is hip stiffness that makes him gear down on comebacks and curls -- he's a stack-and-go vertical weapon more than a sudden separator underneath. The bet is simple: elite quarterback play turns 72nd-percentile explosiveness into the highlight reel the five-star ranking always promised.
style_layers_pending: PBP percentiles are from his 2025 Auburn tape (canonical id 10407); fan-vs-rival discourse descriptors pending discourse-extraction cleanup + in-season Texas data
